steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.Castra: 'Castra' is a neuter plural noun meaning 'camp / military camp'
hints.Italia: 'Italia' is a proper noun — the name of Italy
hints.populum: 'populum' is a noun in the accusative case

hints.to go: 'to go' is 'ire' — 'sunt' is a form of 'esse'

hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'erant' — 'sunt' is present tense
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sint' — 'sunt' is indicative

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry)

hints.Accusative: Neuter plural accusative is the same form, but 'Castra' is the subject of 'sunt'
hints.Ablative: Ablative plural would be 'castris' — 'Castra' is nominative
hints.Vocative: One does not address a camp — 'Castra' is the nominative subject

hints.1st: 1st declension plural nominative ends in -ae — 'castra' is 2nd declension neuter plural (castrum, -i)
hints.3rd: 3rd declension neuter plurals end in -a too, but 'castrum' with gen. 'castri' is 2nd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have gen. in -us — 'castrum' has gen. 'castri' (2nd)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'populus' — 'populum' with -um ending is accusative
hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'populi' — 'populum' is accusative after 'contra'
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'populo' — 'contra' governs the accusative

hints.1st: 1st declension accusative ends in -am — 'populus, -i' is 2nd declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension accusative ends in -em — 'populus, -i' is 2nd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension accusative singular ends in -um too, but 'populus, populi' is 2nd declension

steps.prompt: What is the rhetorical purpose of stating this plain fact?
steps.target: To shock the senate into recognizing the immediate military threat
choices: To shock the senate into recognizing the immediate military threat
To reassure the senate that all is well

hints.To praise Rome's military readiness: The camp is Catiline's — it is an enemy camp threatening Rome, not a Roman defense
hints.To describe routine military exercises: A camp 'against the Roman people' is treasonous, not routine
hints.To reassure the senate that all is well: The opposite — Cicero is alarming the senate about the danger they are ignoring

translation: There is a military camp in Italy against the Roman people.
